_read_this: Ground truth for the synthetic fixture. It exists to MEASURE the extraction, not to feed it. Never pass this file to the model as a hint — that would test its ability to copy, not to read.
_note: Label values are drawn as pixels inside the PNG and are intentionally Portuguese. Never translate them. That covers every components[].label ("App Mobile", "API Gateway", "Svc Pedidos", "DB Pedidos", "Notificacao" — no cedilla, no tilde, exactly as drawn) and every connections[].label ("REST", "POST", "SQL", "?"). preflight.py --extract scores the vision output against these exact strings: translating one turns a CORRECT reading into a hallucination.
nature: synthetic, shaky strokes, imitates a hand-drawn sketch. Correct source_kind: napkin

connections.TRAP: Line with NO arrowhead, labeled '?'. It is the central test of this fixture.

pass_criteria: 1. All 5 components show up, with roughly correct labels.
2. The 3 arrows that do have arrowheads show up with the CORRECT direction (reversing one is the classic failure).
3. The 4th line becomes sync='unknown' plus an unknowns[] entry with blocking=true. If the model asserts a direction here, it MADE IT UP — the fixture does not carry that information.
4. No component that is not in the list above (Redis, Kafka, a queue... = pattern hallucination).
5. Running --gate, the AIR must be BLOCKED because of the unknown from item 3.

what_each_failure_means.reversed an arrow: weak extraction prompt, or a rotated image — check the EXIF rotation
what_each_failure_means.asserted a direction on the line with no: the model is completing the drawing; the EXTRACT_PROMPT is not being obeyed
what_each_failure_means.invented a component: same thing — and it is the most dangerous error, because it is plausible
what_each_failure_means.high confidence on everything: poor calibration; a napkin should push confidence down
